Case study

Research & Design

Role

Lead Designer
Lead User Researcher

Company

Moody's Analytics
Projection Tool

Timeline

2023, 1–2 weeks

Users impacted

200+ global analysts

TLDR

Analysts kept telling us the tool felt overwhelming. I ran the research, identified what was actually causing it, and designed a density feature that gave them control, shipped to 200+ analysts who use this tool every day.

The problem

Product leadership wanted a redesign. I wanted answers first.

The Moody's Projection Tool is used daily by financial analysts worldwide to model complex debt structures. Product leadership had been fielding a wave of vague, frustrated feedback: analysts were saying the tool had "too much whitespace" and felt overwhelming. The pressure was on to act fast.

Leadership's instinct was a structural redesign. I pushed back. A full redesign based on unvalidated assumptions was a big risk. I wanted to avoid wasting time and resources on solving the wrong problem. I made the case for running research sessions first so we could understand what analysts were actually experiencing.

Research

I had 1 week. I led 7 user interviews. I made 3 discoveries.

I negotiated one week to run research before any design decisions were made. I held 7 user interviews with analysts, working to understand what was actually happening in their day-to-day use of the tool. Three patterns emerged.

1
Disruption caused friction
Analysts were being forced by Moody's to move away from Excel and into a new tool with new limitations. A lot of the negative feedback came from this underlying frustration with the transition itself.
2
Physical artifacts needed work
Analysts preferred to bring physical printouts of their models to certain meetings. The digital tools enabled PDF exports, but the formatting needed work to improve readability in print.

This became a separate work item: we reformatted the PDF export templates to better support printout use cases, improving column spacing, type legibility and page breaks.

3
Overwhelming amounts of data
In Excel, analysts could customize their models to only show data relevant to the company they were analyzing. Without this ability, rows and rows of unused data cluttered their views.

The key insight: "Too much white space" didn't mean we needed to redesign. It meant we needed to consider what content was taking up unnecessary space in the viewport.

The third finding pointed most directly to a solution. Analysts shared that in Excel, they had been able to customize their models to only show the data that was relevant to the company they were analyzing. Without having that capability in the new tool, they found themselves scrolling through large collections of unused rows in the table before they could get to information that actually mattered to them. That whitespace wasn't a design problem. It was a data visibility problem, and that distinction mattered. It pointed toward a targeted feature rather than a wholesale redesign.

Explorations

I scheduled follow-up sessions to test three concepts.

With that reframe in hand, I worked with engineering to understand what was technically feasible. Historical data in the tool meant the system could identify which rows had never been filled in by an analyst for a given issuer, and mark those as irrelevant. That gave us a real mechanism to hide unused rows. I then tested three approaches to surfacing that control.

Toggle above the table

Analysts flagged this as too prominent for a one-and-done action. Setting unused rows is something you configure once, not something you reach for repeatedly. A persistent control above the table felt like too much real estate for a rarely needed action.

Automatically collapsed rows

Technically compelling, but raised a usability concern. For a tool where users need full confidence in what they are seeing, hiding data without an obvious way to get it back felt risky.

Toggle in the command palette (selected)

The command palette was already the home for view controls (full screen, show and hide account codes), so adding the unused rows toggle here matched existing mental models. Analysts found it intuitively, and it kept the table header clean.

Three concepts tested with analysts in follow-up usability sessions

After 5 usability tests, the command palette placement was the clear winner. It fit an existing mental model without adding noise to the table header. Analysts who already knew how to access view controls found it naturally.

Final design

Verified by engineering and product before shipping.

Before moving to build, I aligned with engineering and product on the command palette direction. The implementation was straightforward. The toggle slotted into an existing pattern in the codebase, and the feature shipped in a single sprint.

Closed: command palette icon visible in toolbar

Open: Show Unused Rows toggle active

Outcome

Shipped in one sprint. Usage spiked immediately.

The feature was implemented and launched in one sprint. Fullstory tracking reported a 60% increase in user clicks on the component after launch. Because the feature only needs to be activated once, the spike represents sustained adoption rather than repeat use.

More tellingly: in follow-up research sessions after launch, whitespace no longer came up as a pain point. The problem that had triggered a near-redesign had been solved with a single, targeted feature.

0
whitespace complaints raised in post-launch research sessions
5
usability sessions run, with unanimous preference for the command palette placement
60%
increase in user clicks on the component, representing sustained long-term adoption

Looking back, I wish we had set a baseline for user engagement metrics before shipping. We didn't have pre-launch data on how often analysts were scrolling past unused rows, so quantifying the improvement afterward was harder than it needed to be. If I were running this project again, I'd make sure that baseline was in place before the research sprint even started.
